goal: Prepare for a certification exam next month (3-hour exam, mixed MCQ and short essays).
constraints: Format: mixed MCQ and short essays
Resources: access to all class materials
Weekends should be dedicated to deep work and simulations
Longer blocks on weekends with mornings reserved for hardest topics
Alternate modality on weekends: deep study → timed practice → review
Recovery and breaks scheduled to prevent fatigue
decisions: Week 1: diagnostic with one past paper under timed conditions
Interleave topic reviews with targeted past-paper questions mapping to each topic
Mid-prep: start timed sections (MCQ-only, essay-only) from past papers; plan a full timed mock in Week 3
Final week: do 2–3 full past exams spaced 2–3 days apart with error review
Aim for 4 full-length, timed practice exams across the four weeks (plus optional 1–2 extras if bandwidth)
Weekdays: 1–2 outlines per day; 1 short full essay midweek; Weekends: 1–2 full timed essays and several outlines

Topic load rule: Weekdays = 1 major topic + 1 minor topic per day (2 topics total); optional up to 3 if a major topic splits naturally. Weekends = 2 major topics + 1 mini/practice block per day (3–4 topics total).
Revision policy for known material: predominantly quick recall with periodic full problems to test application.
Daily quick reviews: 10–20 min on weekdays; 1–2 short drills (30–45 min) on selected weekdays.
Weekly full problem blocks: 60–120 min that include known topics to test application under pressure.
Mastery-based downgrade: if you score ≥85–90% on mixed problems twice in a row and can explain solutions aloud in 1–2 minutes, reduce review frequency to weekly checks.
Error-log protocol: for a careless error, spend 5–10 minutes logging the error and redo the problem immediately.
Conceptual error protocol: treat as a weak topic; redo the one-sheet, complete 3–5 targeted problems, and schedule a re-check in 2–3 days.
Spacing rhythm: after mastery, review next day, then at 3, 7, and 14 days.
Hard topics should be tackled at your peak energy; place 1–2 hardest tasks at the start of the first study block.
Limit hard-topic count to no more than two heavy problems/topics per day; use 50–90 minute blocks for hard work.
Spread revisiting difficult material across the week rather than clustering it all on one day.
entities: Major topic definition: deep-study topic (60–120+ minutes) such as ARIMA modeling, unit-root tests, or power analysis.
Minor topic definition: quick review/subtopic (30–45 minutes) such as reading ACF, p-value interpretation, or short regression diagnostics.
one low-cognitive recharge day per week
open: Remediation plan for consistent gaps (60-90 minute session)
